channel.title: ThingSource — Curious Origins & Accidental Genius
channel.description: An automated daily blog researching the surprising origin of everyday things.

item.title: Caesar Salad Has Nothing to Do With Julius Caesar — The Real Story Is Way Better
item.description: Almost everyone assumes the Caesar salad is named after the ancient Roman emperor, but it was actually invented in a Tijuana restaurant in 1924 by an Italian immigrant scrambling to feed a hungry crowd with almost nothing in his kitchen. The true origin is a chaotic, boozy, borderline-accidental moment of culinary genius that the food world largely forgot to tell you.

item.title: The Caesar Salad Was Invented During a Kitchen Emergency in Tijuana
item.description: The world's most iconic salad wasn't crafted by a culinary genius in a French kitchen — it was thrown together in desperation by an Italian immigrant in a Tijuana restaurant on a busy Fourth of July weekend in 1924. The story of how romaine lettuce, raw egg, and Worcestershire sauce became a global phenomenon is stranger than most people ever suspect.

item.title: The Accidental Condiment: How a Failed Experiment Became Britain's Most Mysterious Sauce
item.description: Worcestershire sauce is one of the world's most beloved condiments, found in kitchens from Tokyo to Texas — but almost nobody knows it was a complete accident that sat fermenting in a cellar for nearly two years before anyone realized it was edible.

item.title: The Sweet Secret: Unwrapping the Surprising True Origin of the Fortune Cookie
item.description: That delightful fortune cookie you crack open after your meal? Its history is far more complex and culturally interwoven than you might think, starting not in China, but with Japanese immigrants in America.
